title: Magic Secret Sharing: Threshold Control of Quantum Computational Power via GHZ Entanglement
abstract: We introduce Magic Secret Sharing (MSS), a quantum cryptographic primitive in which the secret is the computational capability of a quantum state rather than its classical description. In the resource theory of magic, non-stabilizer states fuel universal quantum computation via non-Clifford gates; MSS distributes this resource with an (n-1,n) threshold structure using a pre-shared GHZ state and a single local phase gate P(phi) = diag(1, exp(i*phi)). Any individual party holds the maximally mixed state I/2, with Wigner distance C(I/2) = 0, so no local operation can yield non-Clifford computational advantage regardless of what operations are applied or what noise acts on the device. The authorised coalition reconstructs magic content C(phi) = (|sin(phi)| + |cos(phi)| - 1)/2 exactly, enabling a logical T gate via gate teleportation in multi-server blind quantum computation (BQC). Among diagonal parametric gates, phase gates are the unique class satisfying the security condition, characterised via an exact column-sum condition. The protocol is elevated to a one-sided device-independent (1SDI) setting via a steering inequality: the assemblage produced on the recipient's side certifies magic delivery without trusting the coalition's devices. We demonstrate the (2,3) instance on ibm_marrakesh (156-qubit IBM Heron): security (C(rho_Bob) = 0.000, below LP reconstruction tolerance) holds in all runs, and state fidelity reaches 0.959-0.986 for the authorised party, with faithfulness confirmed for all four test values of phi including near-exact recovery (C = 0.154 vs theory 0.153) for phi = pi/8.
summary: This paper presents 'Magic Secret Sharing,' a method to distribute the non-Clifford resources required for universal quantum computing such that only a specific coalition can access them. By using GHZ states and phase gates, the authors show that computational power can be shared with a threshold structure, even providing a device-independent way to certify the delivery of magic. The protocol was successfully demonstrated on a large-scale IBM quantum processor.
main_problem: The paper introduces a new quantum cryptographic primitive, Magic Secret Sharing (MSS), which aims to distribute the computational capability (non-Cliffordness) of a quantum state using a threshold structure.
main_result: The authors demonstrate a (n-1, n) threshold protocol where an authorized coalition can reconstruct magic content, while unauthorized parties hold stabilizer states. This was experimentally validated on the 156-qubit IBM Heron processor with high fidelity and near-exact magic recovery.
method: The protocol uses GHZ states and local phase gates, employing the resource theory of magic (Wigner distance) to quantify non-stabilizerness. Certification is achieved via one-sided device-independent (1SDI) steering inequalities.
model_or_system: The protocol is implemented on the 'ibm_marrakesh' (156-qubit IBM Heron r2) quantum hardware using a 3-party (2,3) GHZ state configuration.
key_observables: Magic content (Wigner distance C), state fidelity, security metric (C of the unauthorized party), and steering inequality violations.
important_parameters: The phase parameter phi (controlling magic injection), the (n-1, n) threshold structure, and the 15-to-1 magic state distillation threshold.
main_assumptions: The security relies on the invariance of the maximally mixed state under local unitaries; the 1SDI setting assumes the recipient does not need to trust the coalition's devices.
figures_summary: Figure 2(a) is mentioned in the notes, and Table 1 summarizes experimental results including theoretical vs. measured magic content, error bars, and security metrics.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the MSS primitive, defines the resource theory framework, provides mathematical proofs for the threshold structure and gate uniqueness, presents the 1SDI certification method, and concludes with experimental validation on IBM hardware.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: (2, 3) MSS circuit. Alice (q0) prepares a GHZ state (H + two CX gates), injects magic via P(φ), and measures X, broadcasting mA. Bob (q1) measures X and broadcasts mB. Charlie (q2) applies ZmB to obtain P(φ)|+⟩with C = C(φ).

Figure 2: Standard MSS on ibm marrakesh (4096 shots). (a) Measured C(ρC) (scatter, 1σ bootstrap error bars) against the theory curve C(φ) = (| sin φ| + | cos φ| −1)/2 (line). All four φ values are faithful to within 0.034. (b) C(ρB) < 10−6 in every case — noise- robust security from the I/2 invariance argument.
